content.claim: Hungary shows the highest level of socioeconomic segregation of students between schools in the EU (2022 PISA), with between-school variance explaining 71% of reading-performance differences (vs. 36% OECD average); Roma pupil segregation is the second-highest in the region at 62% of Roma children in majority-Roma schools. Early selective tracks are identified as one structural amplifier alongside free primary-school choice.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Demography lens
limitations: Segregation is documented as correlational; researchers do not claim 6/8-year tracks alone cause national segregation pattern
Free primary-school choice cited as equally important driver but causal decomposition between the two mechanisms not quantified
Within-school heterogeneity or composition changes over time not addressed
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
